content.text:
Base directory for this skill: /home/agent/.claude/skills/start-implementation

## Environment

### Required (resolved via `resolve-project-env`)
| Variable | Description |
|---|---|
| `SSH_KEY_PATH` | SSH private key for git clone/fetch/push |
| `GIT_AUTHOR_NAME` | Actor recorded in task log |
| `GIT_AUTHOR_EMAIL` | Actor recorded in task log |
| `<REPO_ID_UPPER>_LOCAL_PATH` | Local path to each repo used by tasks in this workspace |

---

## Must resolve environment first

Before any git or repo operation, invoke:

- `resolve-project-env`

Required outputs:

- validated `project_root`
- resolved `SSH_KEY_PATH`
- resolved git identity values
- resolved repo local path values

If required values are missing, require the user to provide them. Do not guess.

## Must verify

- task file exists
- task status is `ready` — **hard stop** if status is anything else (including `todo`, `in_progress`, `blocked`). Print the current status and stop. Do not proceed.
- all tasks in `depends_on` are `done`
- repo matches `workspace.yaml -> repos[].id`
- repo local path resolves correctly
- working tree is clean

## Git / SSH rule

When repo access requires SSH:

- use the SSH key resolved from project `.env` via `SSH_KEY_PATH`
- do not assume the default SSH key is correct

## Must then

Resolve the base branch from `workspace.yaml -> repos[].base_branch` for the task's repo (required; unset is an error).

### Implementation repo

1. **Fetch from remote** — never skip this; a stale local branch is not acceptable:
   ```
   git fetch origin
   ```
   **Hard stop** if this fails — set `status: blocked`, set `blocked_reason` to the git error message, append a log entry (action: `blocked`, note: the git error message, timestamp: real local time), and stop. Do not proceed.

2. **Checkout and hard-reset to the remote base branch** — this is the only safe starting point:
   ```
   git checkout <base_branch>
   git reset --hard origin/<base_branch>
   ```
   **Hard stop** if either command fails — set `status: blocked`, set `blocked_reason` to the git error message, append a log entry, and stop.

3. **Create the feature branch**:
   ```
   git checkout -b feature/<feature_id>-<work_id>
   ```
   **Hard stop** if this fails — set `status: blocked`, set `blocked_reason` to the git error message, append a log entry, and stop.

   If the feature branch already exists locally (e.g. from a previous aborted attempt), delete it first:
   ```
   git branch -D feature/<feature_id>-<work_id>
   ```
   then repeat steps 2–3.

### Management repo

The task branch already exists on the management repo — `claimTask` created and pushed it as part of the claim. Do **not** create it again.

1. **Fetch from remote**:
   ```
   git fetch origin
   ```
   **Hard stop** if this fails — set `status: blocked`, append a log entry, and stop.

2. **Checkout and hard-reset to the existing task branch**:
   ```
   git checkout feature/<feature_id>-<work_id>
   git reset --hard origin/feature/<feature_id>-<work_id>
   ```
   **Hard stop** if either command fails (e.g. branch unexpectedly missing on remote) — set `status: blocked`, append a log entry, and stop. Do not proceed.

## Must append task log

- action: started
- actor: resolved `GIT_AUTHOR_EMAIL`
- timestamp: real local time via `date +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S%z` — never hardcode
- `rag_context_injected`: `false` at start time (updated to `true` after step 2b if RAG context was successfully injected)

### Task log entry format

Every log entry produced during an agent work phase must follow this schema:

```yaml
log:
  - action: started            # or work_phase_complete, blocked, etc.
    by: <GIT_AUTHOR_EMAIL>
    at: <ISO 8601 timestamp>
    note: <optional free-text note>
    rag_context_injected: false  # true if rag_query returned ≥1 chunk and was appended to agentContext
    tokens:
      input: 0                 # input tokens used in this work phase (0 at start, filled at completion)
      output: 0                # output tokens generated
      total: 0                 # input + output
      cost_usd: 0.000          # estimated USD cost at current model rates
```

`rag_context_injected` and `tokens` are **required fields** on every agent-produced log entry. Set `rag_context_injected: false` and all `tokens` values to `0` at the `started` entry; update them in the completion log entry once the work phase ends. If token usage is unavailable (e.g. the runtime does not expose it), set all token values to `0` rather than omitting the field.

---

## Step 2b — RAG context injection (required — skip only on tool failure)

After the branch is set up and before beginning implementation, you **must** attempt RAG context injection. Do not skip this step unless the tool call itself fails.

1. **Check whether `mcp__rag-server__rag_query` is available** — if the tool does not appear in your tool list (MCP server not connected), skip silently and proceed. Do not warn or error.

2. **Call `mcp__rag-server__rag_query`** using these exact arguments:
   ```
   query:        "<task_title> <feature_id>"
   workspace_id: <workspace.yaml -> workspace_id>
   top_k:        5
   ```
   If the tool call fails (error response, timeout), skip silently — never crash or block.

3. **Format the results** — build a `## Relevant project context` section from the returned chunks:
   - Each chunk: `### <source_type>: <source_path>\n<content>`
   - Cap the entire section at **~500 tokens** (approximately 375 words)
   - Truncate at the nearest chunk boundary — never truncate mid-chunk or mid-sentence
   - If all chunks fit within the cap, include them all
   - If no chunks are returned (empty index), omit the section entirely

4. **Use the results** — read the returned chunks before reading the task spec. They provide baseline context (prior decisions, design notes, related code) that shapes how you implement the task.

5. **Set `rag_context_injected`** — record `true` if at least one chunk was successfully retrieved; `false` otherwise (tool unavailable or call failed). This value is written into the task log entry.

**Graceful degradation guarantee:** If the MCP tool is absent or the call fails, proceed exactly as if this step did not exist. No crash, no warning, no change in behavior — just cold start.

---

## Must then implement, self-review, and open the PR

After the branch is created, the `started` log entry is written, and RAG context has been injected (or skipped), proceed directly through the full execution contract **without pausing for human confirmation**:

### Step 1 — Implement

Read the following documents **in order** before writing any code:

1. `docs/features/<feature_id>/product-spec.md` — understand the original user requirements and business constraints
2. `docs/features/<feature_id>/technical-design.md` — understand the implementation approach
3. `tasks.md` (under `## T<n> — <title>`) — understand the specific task scope and subtasks

Make all required code and documentation changes in the implementation repo. Commit incrementally as logical units. Push each commit to the feature branch.

Update `task.execution.last_updated_by` and `task.execution.last_updated_at` in the task YAML as work progresses.

#### Format before every commit

Before running `git commit`, always run the project's format command to ensure committed code is properly formatted. Detect the formatter from the project:

- If `package.json` has a `format` script → `yarn format` or `npm run format`
- If `package.json` has a `lint:fix` or `eslint` script → `yarn lint:fix` or `npm run lint:fix`
- If `Makefile` has a `fmt` or `format` target → `make fmt` or `make format`
- If the repo is Go → `gofmt -w .`
- If the repo is Python and has a `pyproject.toml` or `.ruff.toml` → `ruff format .`; otherwise `black .`

If no format command is found, skip silently. Do not fail or block — formatting is best-effort. Stage any files changed by the formatter before committing.

### Step 2 — Self-review

Invoke `pr-self-review` on the completed branch. Fix any blocking issues found. Re-commit and re-push. Repeat until no blocking issues remain.

### Step 3 — Execute the test plan

Read the test plan from the PR description template or from `tasks.md` under `## T<n>`. Execute every item in the test plan:

- For items that can be run (compile checks, unit tests, linters): run them and record actual output.
- For items that require code inspection (config file verification, logic traces): read the relevant files and verify each claim explicitly.
- If a test plan item fails: fix the issue, re-commit, re-push, then re-run the full test plan from scratch.
- **Hard stop** if a test plan item cannot be made to pass after 3 attempts — set `status: blocked`, write `blocked_reason`, append a log entry, and stop. Do not open a PR for failing tests.

After all items pass, record the results (pass/fail per item, test suite counts if applicable) to post as a PR comment in Step 4.

### Step 4 — Create PR

Invoke `pr-create` to push the final branch state and open the pull request. The task status will be set to `in_review` by `pr-create`. Immediately after the PR is created, post the test plan results as a comment on the PR.

### Autonomous execution rules

- Do not ask for confirmation between steps.
- Do not stop after branch setup and wait.
- If a blocking issue arises during implementation that cannot be resolved (ambiguous spec, missing dependency, repeated test failure after 3 attempts), follow the **blocking exit sequence** below and exit — do not open a PR for broken work.

**Blocking exit sequence:**
1. Commit any partial work on the **implementation repo** task branch:
   ```
   git add -A && git commit -m "wip: partial work before block — <brief summary>"
   git push origin feature/<feature_id>-<work_id>
   ```
   If there is nothing to commit (no changes since last commit), skip the commit but still push to ensure the branch is current on remote.
2. Capture the WIP SHA from the implementation repo:
   ```
   git rev-parse HEAD
   ```
3. Write `blocked_context` to the task YAML:
   ```yaml
   blocked_context:
     wip_branch: feature/<feature_id>-<work_id>
     wip_sha: <SHA from step 2>
     pushed_at: <ISO 8601 timestamp via date +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S%z>
   ```
4. Set `status: blocked`, write `blocked_reason` and `suggested_next_step`, append a `blocked` log entry with a real local timestamp.
5. Commit and push the updated task YAML to the **management repo** task branch.

- All four steps (implement → self-review → test plan → PR) are part of a single `/start-implementation` invocation.

---

## Re-do mode

Triggered when the task status is `in_review` and the PR has been rejected or has review comments to address.

### When to use

Use re-do mode instead of the normal flow when:
- the task status is `in_review` (not `ready`)
- the user explicitly asks to fix review comments or re-do the task

### Must verify (re-do)

- task file exists
- task status is `in_review`
- task has a PR field with a valid PR reference
- repo local path resolves correctly

### Must then (re-do)

1. **Update the branch** — fetch remote state first, then check out and fast-forward the feature branch:
   ```
   git fetch origin
   git checkout feature/<feature_id>-<work_id>
   git reset --hard origin/feature/<feature_id>-<work_id>
   ```
   **Hard stop** if any of these commands fail (e.g. branch does not exist on remote) — append a log entry to the task file (action: `blocked`, note: the git error message) and stop. Do not proceed.

2. **Check existing changes** — review what is already on the branch vs the repo's base branch (resolved from `workspace.yaml -> repos[].base_branch` for the task's repo):
   ```
   git diff <base_branch>...HEAD --stat
   git log <base_branch>..HEAD --oneline
   ```

3. **Fetch PR review comments** — retrieve all review comments from the PR using the GitHub API with `GITHUB_TOKEN` from project `.env`:
   ```
   GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/pulls/{pull_number}/comments
   GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/pulls/{pull_number}/reviews
   ```
   Read and understand every comment before making any changes.

4. **Fix the comments** — apply all required changes to resolve the review feedback.

5. **Format and commit** — run the project's format command (see "Format before every commit" rule above), stage any formatter changes, then commit and push:
   ```
   git add <changed files>
   git commit -m "fix: address PR review comments (<summary>)"
   git push origin feature/<feature_id>-<work_id>
   ```

6. **Mark in_review again** — update the task log with the fix action and ensure status remains `in_review`.

### Must append task log (re-do)

- action: fixed review comments
- PR comments addressed (brief summary)
- actor: resolved `GIT_AUTHOR_EMAIL`
- timestamp: real local time via `date +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S%z` — never hardcode

---

## Blocked recovery mode

Triggered when task `status === "ready"` AND `task.blocked_context` is non-null. Use this mode instead of normal branch setup when **both** conditions are true.

### When to use

- task status is `ready`
- `task.blocked_context` is non-null (a prior run committed partial WIP work)

### Must then (recovery)

1. **Fetch origin on both repos**:
   ```
   git fetch origin   # run on both implementation repo and management repo
   ```

2. **Management repo — checkout the WIP branch**:
   ```
   git checkout <blocked_context.wip_branch>
   git reset --hard origin/<blocked_context.wip_branch>
   ```
   **Hard stop** if either command fails — set `status: blocked`, append a log entry, and stop.

3. **Implementation repo — checkout the same WIP branch**:
   ```
   git checkout <blocked_context.wip_branch>
   git reset --hard origin/<blocked_context.wip_branch>
   ```
   **Hard stop** if either command fails — set `status: blocked`, append a log entry, and stop.

4. **Read prior context** — read `blocked_reason` and `suggested_next_step` from the task YAML to understand why the prior run stopped. Use this as additional context before continuing implementation.

5. **Append a `recovery_started` log entry** to the task YAML with a real local timestamp. Commit and push this to the management repo task branch.

6. **Proceed with implementation** — continue from where the prior run left off. The partial WIP commits are already on the branch.

7. **On successful PR creation** — set `blocked_context: null` in the task YAML, commit the change, and push to the task branch before the PR is opened.

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: /home/agent/.claude/skills/resolve-project-env

## Environment

### Required
| Variable | Description |
|---|---|
| `WORKSPACE_ROOT` | Root that contains `workflow/` and all project folders |
| `GIT_AUTHOR_NAME` | Git commit identity; used as approval actor name |
| `GIT_AUTHOR_EMAIL` | Referenced directly in `workspace.yaml` as `actor_source` |
| `GITHUB_ACCOUNT` | GitHub username or org; required for PR creation and SSH remote URLs |
| `SSH_KEY_PATH` | SSH private key path; must be explicit — do not assume a default |

### Required per repo (from `workspace.yaml` repos list)
| Variable pattern | Description |
|---|---|
| `<REPO_ID_UPPER>_LOCAL_PATH` | Filesystem path to the local clone of each repo |

### Optional
| Variable | Default | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `SKIP_STAGING` | `false` | Set to `true` if project has no staging environment |

Base branches are declared per-repo in `workspace.yaml -> repos[].base_branch`, not in `.env`. There is no global default.

---

## Purpose
Provide one shared environment-resolution contract for all workflow skills.

## Inputs
Optional:
- `project_root`

## Path resolution
1. If `project_root` is provided, use it
2. Otherwise use the current directory
3. Validate project root contains:
   - `workspace.yaml`
   - `CLAUDE.md`

If project root cannot be validated, require the user to provide the project folder path.

## Environment resolution
Read `<project_root>/.env` if it exists.

Resolve workflow-relevant values from `.env`, especially:
- `WORKSPACE_ROOT`
- `GIT_AUTHOR_NAME`
- `GIT_AUTHOR_EMAIL`
- `GITHUB_ACCOUNT`
- `SSH_KEY_PATH`

Also resolve any repo local path env references used by `workspace.yaml`, for example:
- `PROJECT_A_API_LOCAL_PATH`
- `PROJECT_A_WEB_LOCAL_PATH`
- `PROJECT_A_DATA_LOCAL_PATH`

## Rules
- Do not guess missing required values
- If a required value is missing, ask the user explicitly
- Prefer project `.env` over assumptions
- Treat `SSH_KEY_PATH` as required for SSH-based repo access
- Do not assume the default SSH key is correct

## Output
Return a resolved environment summary suitable for downstream workflow skills:
- validated `project_root`
- resolved `WORKSPACE_ROOT`
- resolved git identity values
- resolved `SSH_KEY_PATH`
- resolved repo local path values

content.content:
1	# Technical Design
2	
3	## Feature
4	- Feature ID: `feature-status-dashboard-v2`
5	- Title: `Feature Status Dashboard v2 — Web UI for Workspace Management`
6	
7	---
8	
9	## 1. Current State
10	
11	### File-driven workflow system
12	
13	The agent workflow system is file-driven. The management repo stores:
14	
15	- `workspace.yaml` — workspace config, repos, roles
16	- `docs/features/<id>/status.yaml` — per-feature lifecycle and stage review state
17	- `docs/features/<id>/product-spec.md`, `technical-design.md`, `tasks.md` — narrative artifacts
18	- `docs/features/<id>/tasks/T<n>.yaml` — per-task machine-readable state
19	
20	Humans interact with this system by:
21	- Running CLI skills (`/tech-lead`, `/approve-feature`, `/start-implementation`, etc.)
22	- Editing YAML files directly with a text editor
23	- Running `git` commands to commit and push state changes
24	
25	### v1 — existing `digital-factory-ui` app
26	
27	A first-generation dashboard (v1) already exists in the `digital-factory-ui` repo. It provides a basic visual surface but falls short of the full requirements:
28	
29	- **Incomplete screen coverage** — not all five screens (Workspace Picker, Dashboard, Features, Feature Detail, Task Board) are fully implemented or consistent with the current Figma design.
30	- **Outdated stack** — v1 predates the Next.js 16 / HeroUI v3 / Tailwind CSS v4 decisions; upgrading in-place would be as expensive as a clean rewrite.
31	- **No write-back** — v1 is read-only; it does not implement Approve/Reject/Reset Server Actions or the New Feature scaffold.
32	- **No multi-workspace support** — v1 is hard-coded to a single workspace path.
33	- **No docker-compose integration** — v1 has no Dockerfile or service entry.
34	
35	**v2 is a complete replacement of v1.** The `digital-factory-ui` repo will be rewritten from scratch on a new branch; the v1 codebase is archived before T1 begins.
36	
37	**Relevant repo boundary:** The app lives in the standalone `digital-factory-ui` repo (GitHub: `tiendv89/digital-factory-ui`). The management repo remains the file-system source of truth — the UI reads and writes its YAML files on the local filesystem.
38	
39	---
40	
41	## 2. Problem Framing
42	
43	**What must change (replacing v1):**
44	- The v1 codebase is archived to a `v1-archive` branch before T1 begins. The `main` branch of `digital-factory-ui` receives a clean v2 scaffold.
45	- v2 must provide all five screens (Workspace Picker, Dashboard, Features list, Feature Detail, Task Board) from the Figma design — fully, not partially.
46	- The app must read live data from workspace YAML files (no caching layer, no database).
47	- Approve/Reject/Reset Server Actions must write back to `status.yaml` and commit to the feature's branch — v1 had no write-back; v2 must have it.
48	- The New Feature modal must scaffold the `docs/features/<id>/` directory structure on disk.
49	- Multi-workspace support via `WORKSPACE_SCAN_ROOT` — v1 lacked this.
50	- A `Dockerfile` and docker-compose service entry — v1 had neither.
51	
52	**What must remain stable:**
53	- YAML file schemas — v2 must not change the shape of `status.yaml` or `tasks/T<n>.yaml`.
54	- Workflow rule enforcement — the UI surfaces actions permitted by the rules; it does not bypass them (e.g. it does not mark tasks `done`).
55	- Git as the system of record — all writes go to the correct feature branch via git commit.
56	
57	**Fixed assumptions:**
58	- Single-user, local tool only. No authentication or multi-user support.
59	- Desktop-first at 1440px width. No mobile/responsive breakpoints.
60	- The app runs as a local Next.js dev server (`pnpm dev`) against the host filesystem.
61	- A `WORKSPACE_SCAN_ROOT` environment variable points to the directory containing workspace subdirectories.
62	
63	---
64	
65	## 3. Options Considered
66	
67	### Option A: Electron app (packaged desktop)
68	
69	**What it is:** A fully packaged desktop app with direct Node.js file system access, bundled with Chromium.
70	
71	**Pros:**
72	- Direct `fs` access to YAML files — no server needed
73	- Can spawn `git` subprocesses for commit/push natively
74	- Standalone app, no local server to manage
75	
76	**Cons:**
77	- Significantly heavier build pipeline (electron-builder, code signing)
78	- Slower DX iteration cycle — requires rebuild for code changes
79	- No HMR without extra tooling
80	- Overkill for a single-user developer tool; packaging overhead adds friction to CI
81	
82	**Implementation impact:** Separate Electron main process + renderer process architecture; IPC bridge for file operations
83	**Dependency impact:** Adds electron, electron-builder; significant devDependency footprint
84	
85	---
86	
87	### Option B: Vite + React SPA + Express/Fastify backend
88	
89	**What it is:** A decoupled frontend SPA (Vite + React) talking to a lightweight Node.js HTTP server that handles filesystem reads/writes and git operations.
90	
91	**Pros:**
92	- Clean frontend/backend separation
93	- Vite HMR is fast
94	- Familiar REST API mental model
95	
96	**Cons:**
97	- Two processes to run and keep in sync
98	- More boilerplate than necessary for a local tool
99	- API route management overhead
100	- No shared types without additional tooling (e.g. tRPC)
101	
102	**Implementation impact:** Two packages in a monorepo or two repos; CORS config; runtime glue
103	**Dependency impact:** Adds express/fastify + separate server entry point
104	
105	---
106	
107	### Option C: Next.js 16 App Router (chosen)
108	
109	**What it is:** A single Next.js 16 (App Router) project where Server Components read YAML files on the server, Server Actions handle all writes (status.yaml updates, git commits, directory scaffolding), and the client manages UI state.
110	
111	**Pros:**
112	- Single process, single dev command (`pnpm dev`)
113	- Server Components access `fs` directly — no API glue
114	- Server Actions eliminate the need for explicit API routes for most writes
115	- Strong TypeScript integration, colocated layouts, suspense boundaries
116	- HeroUI v3 + Tailwind CSS v4 fit cleanly into the Next.js component model
117	- Excellent DX with HMR for both server and client components
118	
119	**Cons:**
120	- React Server Components mental model has a learning curve (server vs client component boundary)
121	- Long-running git operations (commit, push) inside Server Actions need explicit timeout handling
122	
123	**Implementation impact:** Standalone repo `digital-factory-ui` (`tiendv89/digital-factory-ui`); single `pnpm dev`; env vars configure workspace root; added to local `docker-compose.yml` for optional containerised startup
124	**Dependency impact:** Next.js 16, React 19, Tailwind CSS v4, HeroUI v3, js-yaml, simple-git
125	
126	---
127	
128	## 4. Chosen Design
129	
130	**Selected approach: Option C — Next.js 16 App Router**
131	
132	**Why:**
133	- A single process running on localhost is the simplest possible deployment for a local dev tool.
134	- Server Components + Server Actions give direct filesystem access without an additional API server.
135	- Next.js App Router's file-based routing maps 1:1 to the screen hierarchy (`/`, `/features`, `/features/[featureId]`, `/tasks`).
136	- HeroUI v3 + Tailwind CSS v4 are a natural match for the Figma design tokens (utility-first, component-driven).
137	
138	### Stack
139	
140	| Layer | Technology |
141	|---|---|
142	| Framework | Next.js 16 (App Router) |
143	| Language | TypeScript (strict) |
144	| Styling | Tailwind CSS v4 |
145	| Component library | HeroUI v3 |
146	| YAML parsing | js-yaml |
147	| Git operations | simple-git |
148	| Fonts | Inter (body), Cousine (mono) via `next/font` |
149	| Runtime | Node.js 20+ |
150	
151	### Architecture
152	
153	The app is a standalone repo (`digital-factory-ui`):
154	
155	```
156	digital-factory-ui/          # repo root
157	├── app/
158	│   ├── layout.tsx               # Root layout: sidebar + header shell
159	│   ├── page.tsx                 # Dashboard (/), workspace picker on first load
160	│   ├── features/
161	│   │   ├── page.tsx             # Features list (/features)
162	│   │   └── [featureId]/
163	│   │       └── page.tsx         # Feature detail (/features/:featureId)
164	│   └── tasks/
165	│       └── page.tsx             # Task Board (/tasks)
166	├── components/
167	│   ├── layout/                  # Sidebar, Header, WorkspaceSwitcher
168	│   ├── workspace/               # WorkspacePicker, WorkspaceCard
169	│   ├── dashboard/               # StatCard, RecentActivity
170	│   ├── features/                # FeaturesTable, FilterPills, NewFeatureModal
171	│   ├── feature-detail/          # StageStepper, ReviewCard, TaskTable
172	│   └── task-board/              # KanbanBoard, TaskCard, KanbanColumn
173	├── lib/
174	│   ├── workspace.ts             # Workspace discovery (scan WORKSPACE_SCAN_ROOT)
175	│   ├── features.ts              # Read status.yaml, list features
176	│   ├── tasks.ts                 # Read tasks/T<n>.yaml
177	│   └── git.ts                   # simple-git wrapper: commit + push server actions
178	├── actions/
179	│   ├── approve.ts               # Server Action: approve/reject/reset status.yaml
180	│   └── init-feature.ts          # Server Action: scaffold new feature directory
181	├── types/
182	│   ├── workspace.ts             # WorkspaceConfig, WorkspaceSummary
183	│   ├── feature.ts               # FeatureStatus, StageReview
184	│   └── task.ts                  # TaskYaml, TaskStatus
185	├── Dockerfile                   # Production image (node:20-alpine, pnpm start)
186	└── .env.local.example           # Template for WORKSPACE_SCAN_ROOT, git config
187	```
188	
189	### Active workspace state
190	
191	The active workspace is stored in `localStorage` (client-side key: `active_workspace_id`) and in a React context (`WorkspaceContext`) so all views scope data to the selected workspace without a full page reload. On first visit (no stored workspace), the app renders the Workspace Picker instead of the Dashboard.
192	
193	### Write flow (Approve/Reject/Reset)
194	
195	1. User clicks Approve on a Feature Detail review card.
196	2. Client calls a Server Action (`approveStage(featureId, stage)`).
197	3. Server Action:
198	   a. Reads current `status.yaml` with `js-yaml`.
199	   b. Updates `review_status`, `reviewed_by` (from `GIT_AUTHOR_EMAIL` env), `reviewed_at`, `review_history`.
200	   c. Writes updated YAML back to disk.
201	   d. Uses `simple-git` to `git add` + `git commit` on the feature's branch.
202	4. Server Action returns `{ ok: true }` or `{ error: string }`.
203	5. Client invalidates the feature data and re-fetches.
204	
205	### Write flow (New Feature)
206	
207	1. User submits the New Feature modal form.
208	2. Client calls `initFeature(params)` Server Action.
209	3. Server Action creates the directory scaffold under `docs/features/<id>/` (status.yaml template, empty subdirs).
210	4. Commits and pushes to a new feature branch.
211	
212	### Configuration
213	
214	```env
215	# digital-factory-ui/.env.local
216	WORKSPACE_SCAN_ROOT=/Users/pye/code/kitelabs
217	GIT_AUTHOR_NAME=pye
218	GIT_AUTHOR_EMAIL=pentative@gmail.com
219	SSH_KEY_PATH=~/.ssh/id_ed25519
220	```
221	
222	`WORKSPACE_SCAN_ROOT` is scanned recursively (one level deep) for `workspace.yaml` files.
223	
224	When running via docker-compose, `WORKSPACE_SCAN_ROOT` is bind-mounted into the container (e.g. `/workspaces`) and `SSH_KEY_PATH` is bind-mounted from the host's SSH agent socket or key file.
225	
226	---
227	
228	## 5. Dependency Analysis
229	
230	### Internal dependencies
231	
232	| Dependency | Type | Status |
233	|---|---|---|
234	| `digital-factory-ui` repo exists at `env:DIGITAL_FACTORY_UI_LOCAL_PATH` | Repo existence | Must be confirmed before T1 |
235	| `status.yaml` schema is stable | File format contract | Stable — product spec confirmed |
236	| `tasks/T<n>.yaml` schema is stable | File format contract | Stable — workflow rules confirmed |
237	| YAML field names for Approve/Reject/Reset | Write contract | Confirmed from workflow rules |
238	| Feature branch naming convention (`feature/<id>`) | Git convention | Confirmed from workspace.yaml |
239	
240	### External dependencies
241	
242	| Dependency | Type | Notes |
243	|---|---|---|
244	| Node.js 20+ on developer machine | Runtime | Required for Next.js 16 |
245	| pnpm | Package manager | Confirmed |
246	| `simple-git` package | npm package | Mature, no auth complexity for local repos |
247	| `js-yaml` package | npm package | Standard YAML parser |
248	| HeroUI v3 | npm package | Requires Tailwind CSS v4; confirmed available |
249	| Inter + Cousine fonts | Google Fonts via `next/font` | No external service required at runtime |
250	
251	### Blocking decisions
252	
253	| Decision | Status | Required before |
254	|---|---|---|
255	| v1 archive: T1 must tag or branch v1 before wiping — confirm branch name (`v1-archive`) | **Assumed `v1-archive`** — confirm | T1 |
256	| Package manager | **pnpm** — resolved | T1 |
257	| Which `docker-compose.yml` file does T10 add the service to? (`workflow` repo root, or a shared root) | **`workflow` repo root** — resolved (see §7 Repository Impact) | T10 |
258	
259	### Configuration dependencies
260	
261	- `WORKSPACE_SCAN_ROOT` must be set in `.env.local` before the app can discover workspaces.
262	- `GIT_AUTHOR_NAME` and `GIT_AUTHOR_EMAIL` must be set for commit attribution.
263	- `SSH_KEY_PATH` must be accessible for push to origin.
264	- `FIGMA_PERSONAL_ACCESS_TOKEN` is available in project `.env` and will be used during UI implementation per the Figma MCP usage rule.
265	
266	---
267	
268	## 6. Parallelization / Blocking Analysis
269	
270	```
271	D1: Confirm v1-archive branch name and whether v1 has any commits to preserve before wipe
272	  └── Unblock before T1. Check DIGITAL_FACTORY_UI_LOCAL_PATH from .env; inspect main branch.
273	
274	T1: v1 archive + v2 scaffold — tag v1 → wipe → Next.js 16, TypeScript, Tailwind v4, HeroUI v3, design tokens, fonts, Dockerfile
275	  └── Can begin now once D1 is resolved — no other blockers
276	  │
277	  T2: Server data layer — workspace discovery, YAML readers, Server Actions for status writes + git
278	  T3: App shell — sidebar, header, WorkspaceContext provider, nav links, workspace switcher
279	      └── T2 and T3 run in parallel
280	      └── BLOCKED on T1 (project scaffold and dependencies must exist)
281	      │
282	      T4: Workspace picker screen — discovery grid, workspace cards, search filter
283	      T5: Dashboard screen — stat cards, recent activity feed
284	      T6: Features list screen — filterable table, filter pills, status badges, progress bars
285	      T7: Feature detail screen — stage stepper, review card, approve/reject/reset, task table
286	      T8: New feature modal — form, slug auto-gen, validation, directory scaffold action
287	      T9: Task Board screen — kanban columns, task cards, feature/repo/actor filters
288	          └── T4, T5, T6, T7, T8, T9 run in parallel with each other
289	          └── BLOCKED on T2 (data layer and server actions must be available)
290	          └── BLOCKED on T3 (sidebar + header layout shell must exist for consistent page structure)
291	
292	T10: docker-compose — add digital-factory-ui service to docker-compose.yml in workflow repo
293	     └── BLOCKED on T1 (Dockerfile must exist before docker-compose service can be defined)
294	     └── Runs in parallel with T2/T3 and T4-T9 once T1 is done
295	```
296	
297	**Wave summary:**
298	
299	| Wave | Tasks | Parallelism |
300	|---|---|---|
301	| Wave 0 | D1 (unblock decision) | Immediate |
302	| Wave 1 | T1 | Single task |
303	| Wave 2 | T2, T3, T10 | Fully parallel |
304	| Wave 3 | T4, T5, T6, T7, T8, T9 | Fully parallel |
305	
306	---
307	
308	## 7. Repository Impact
309	
310	| Repo | Impact | Reason |
311	|---|---|---|
312	| `digital-factory-ui` | Primary — T1–T9 write here | The web app is this standalone repo |
313	| `workflow` | T10 only — adds docker-compose service entry | docker-compose.yml lives in the workflow repo |
314	| `management-repo` | Read-only at runtime; written by Server Actions via `simple-git` | The app reads and writes `status.yaml`, `tasks/T<n>.yaml`, scaffolds feature dirs |
315	
316	No changes are required to `rag-service`.
317	
318	The `management-repo` is mutated only via `simple-git` running inside Next.js Server Actions — not via direct file writes without git tracking. This ensures all state changes remain committed artifacts.
319	
320	---
321	
322	## 8. Validation and Release Impact
323	
324	### v1 replacement strategy
325	
326	v2 is a full rewrite. The replacement sequence for T1 is:
327	
328	1. **Archive v1** — create a `v1-archive` branch from the current `main` of `digital-factory-ui` and push it. This preserves v1 history without blocking v2.
329	2. **Wipe main** — reset `main` to an empty state (remove all v1 files).
330	3. **Scaffold v2** — initialise a fresh Next.js 16 app at the repo root, add all dependencies, Dockerfile, and `.env.local.example`.
331	4. **Commit v2 scaffold** — commit with message `feat: v2 scaffold — Next.js 16 App Router`.
332

# Technical Design

## Feature
- Feature ID: `feature-status-dashboard-v2`
- Title: `Feature Status Dashboard v2 — Web UI for Workspace Management`

---

## 1. Current State

### File-driven workflow system

The agent workflow system is file-driven. The management repo stores:

- `workspace.yaml` — workspace config, repos, roles
- `docs/features/<id>/status.yaml` — per-feature lifecycle and stage review state
- `docs/features/<id>/product-spec.md`, `technical-design.md`, `tasks.md` — narrative artifacts
- `docs/features/<id>/tasks/T<n>.yaml` — per-task machine-readable state

Humans interact with this system by:
- Running CLI skills (`/tech-lead`, `/approve-feature`, `/start-implementation`, etc.)
- Editing YAML files directly with a text editor
- Running `git` commands to commit and push state changes

### v1 — existing `digital-factory-ui` app

A first-generation dashboard (v1) already exists in the `digital-factory-ui` repo. It provides a basic visual surface but falls short of the full requirements:

- **Incomplete screen coverage** — not all five screens (Workspace Picker, Dashboard, Features, Feature Detail, Task Board) are fully implemented or consistent with the current Figma design.
- **Outdated stack** — v1 predates the Next.js 16 / HeroUI v3 / Tailwind CSS v4 decisions; upgrading in-place would be as expensive as a clean rewrite.
- **No write-back** — v1 is read-only; it does not implement Approve/Reject/Reset Server Actions or the New Feature scaffold.
- **No multi-workspace support** — v1 is hard-coded to a single workspace path.
- **No docker-compose integration** — v1 has no Dockerfile or service entry.

**v2 is a complete replacement of v1.** The `digital-factory-ui` repo will be rewritten from scratch on a new branch; the v1 codebase is archived before T1 begins.

**Relevant repo boundary:** The app lives in the standalone `digital-factory-ui` repo (GitHub: `tiendv89/digital-factory-ui`). The management repo remains the file-system source of truth — the UI reads and writes its YAML files on the local filesystem.

---

## 2. Problem Framing

**What must change (replacing v1):**
- The v1 codebase is archived to a `v1-archive` branch before T1 begins. The `main` branch of `digital-factory-ui` receives a clean v2 scaffold.
- v2 must provide all five screens (Workspace Picker, Dashboard, Features list, Feature Detail, Task Board) from the Figma design — fully, not partially.
- The app must read live data from workspace YAML files (no caching layer, no database).
- Approve/Reject/Reset Server Actions must write back to `status.yaml` and commit to the feature's branch — v1 had no write-back; v2 must have it.
- The New Feature modal must scaffold the `docs/features/<id>/` directory structure on disk.
- Multi-workspace support via `WORKSPACE_SCAN_ROOT` — v1 lacked this.
- A `Dockerfile` and docker-compose service entry — v1 had neither.

**What must remain stable:**
- YAML file schemas — v2 must not change the shape of `status.yaml` or `tasks/T<n>.yaml`.
- Workflow rule enforcement — the UI surfaces actions permitted by the rules; it does not bypass them (e.g. it does not mark tasks `done`).
- Git as the system of record — all writes go to the correct feature branch via git commit.

**Fixed assumptions:**
- Single-user, local tool only. No authentication or multi-user support.
- Desktop-first at 1440px width. No mobile/responsive breakpoints.
- The app runs as a local Next.js dev server (`pnpm dev`) against the host filesystem.
- A `WORKSPACE_SCAN_ROOT` environment variable points to the directory containing workspace subdirectories.

---

## 3. Options Considered

### Option A: Electron app (packaged desktop)

**What it is:** A fully packaged desktop app with direct Node.js file system access, bundled with Chromium.

**Pros:**
- Direct `fs` access to YAML files — no server needed
- Can spawn `git` subprocesses for commit/push natively
- Standalone app, no local server to manage

**Cons:**
- Significantly heavier build pipeline (electron-builder, code signing)
- Slower DX iteration cycle — requires rebuild for code changes
- No HMR without extra tooling
- Overkill for a single-user developer tool; packaging overhead adds friction to CI

**Implementation impact:** Separate Electron main process + renderer process architecture; IPC bridge for file operations
**Dependency impact:** Adds electron, electron-builder; significant devDependency footprint

---

### Option B: Vite + React SPA + Express/Fastify backend

**What it is:** A decoupled frontend SPA (Vite + React) talking to a lightweight Node.js HTTP server that handles filesystem reads/writes and git operations.

**Pros:**
- Clean frontend/backend separation
- Vite HMR is fast
- Familiar REST API mental model

**Cons:**
- Two processes to run and keep in sync
- More boilerplate than necessary for a local tool
- API route management overhead
- No shared types without additional tooling (e.g. tRPC)

**Implementation impact:** Two packages in a monorepo or two repos; CORS config; runtime glue
**Dependency impact:** Adds express/fastify + separate server entry point

---

### Option C: Next.js 16 App Router (chosen)

**What it is:** A single Next.js 16 (App Router) project where Server Components read YAML files on the server, Server Actions handle all writes (status.yaml updates, git commits, directory scaffolding), and the client manages UI state.

**Pros:**
- Single process, single dev command (`pnpm dev`)
- Server Components access `fs` directly — no API glue
- Server Actions eliminate the need for explicit API routes for most writes
- Strong TypeScript integration, colocated layouts, suspense boundaries
- HeroUI v3 + Tailwind CSS v4 fit cleanly into the Next.js component model
- Excellent DX with HMR for both server and client components

**Cons:**
- React Server Components mental model has a learning curve (server vs client component boundary)
- Long-running git operations (commit, push) inside Server Actions need explicit timeout handling

**Implementation impact:** Standalone repo `digital-factory-ui` (`tiendv89/digital-factory-ui`); single `pnpm dev`; env vars configure workspace root; added to local `docker-compose.yml` for optional containerised startup
**Dependency impact:** Next.js 16, React 19, Tailwind CSS v4, HeroUI v3, js-yaml, simple-git

---

## 4. Chosen Design

**Selected approach: Option C — Next.js 16 App Router**

**Why:**
- A single process running on localhost is the simplest possible deployment for a local dev tool.
- Server Components + Server Actions give direct filesystem access without an additional API server.
- Next.js App Router's file-based routing maps 1:1 to the screen hierarchy (`/`, `/features`, `/features/[featureId]`, `/tasks`).
- HeroUI v3 + Tailwind CSS v4 are a natural match for the Figma design tokens (utility-first, component-driven).

### Stack

| Layer | Technology |
|---|---|
| Framework | Next.js 16 (App Router) |
| Language | TypeScript (strict) |
| Styling | Tailwind CSS v4 |
| Component library | HeroUI v3 |
| YAML parsing | js-yaml |
| Git operations | simple-git |
| Fonts | Inter (body), Cousine (mono) via `next/font` |
| Runtime | Node.js 20+ |

### Architecture

The app is a standalone repo (`digital-factory-ui`):

```
digital-factory-ui/          # repo root
├── app/
│   ├── layout.tsx               # Root layout: sidebar + header shell
│   ├── page.tsx                 # Dashboard (/), workspace picker on first load
│   ├── features/
│   │   ├── page.tsx             # Features list (/features)
│   │   └── [featureId]/
│   │       └── page.tsx         # Feature detail (/features/:featureId)
│   └── tasks/
│       └── page.tsx             # Task Board (/tasks)
├── components/
│   ├── layout/                  # Sidebar, Header, WorkspaceSwitcher
│   ├── workspace/               # WorkspacePicker, WorkspaceCard
│   ├── dashboard/               # StatCard, RecentActivity
│   ├── features/                # FeaturesTable, FilterPills, NewFeatureModal
│   ├── feature-detail/          # StageStepper, ReviewCard, TaskTable
│   └── task-board/              # KanbanBoard, TaskCard, KanbanColumn
├── lib/
│   ├── workspace.ts             # Workspace discovery (scan WORKSPACE_SCAN_ROOT)
│   ├── features.ts              # Read status.yaml, list features
│   ├── tasks.ts                 # Read tasks/T<n>.yaml
│   └── git.ts                   # simple-git wrapper: commit + push server actions
├── actions/
│   ├── approve.ts               # Server Action: approve/reject/reset status.yaml
│   └── init-feature.ts          # Server Action: scaffold new feature directory
├── types/
│   ├── workspace.ts             # WorkspaceConfig, WorkspaceSummary
│   ├── feature.ts               # FeatureStatus, StageReview
│   └── task.ts                  # TaskYaml, TaskStatus
├── Dockerfile                   # Production image (node:20-alpine, pnpm start)
└── .env.local.example           # Template for WORKSPACE_SCAN_ROOT, git config
```

### Active workspace state

The active workspace is stored in `localStorage` (client-side key: `active_workspace_id`) and in a React context (`WorkspaceContext`) so all views scope data to the selected workspace without a full page reload. On first visit (no stored workspace), the app renders the Workspace Picker instead of the Dashboard.

### Write flow (Approve/Reject/Reset)

1. User clicks Approve on a Feature Detail review card.
2. Client calls a Server Action (`approveStage(featureId, stage)`).
3. Server Action:
   a. Reads current `status.yaml` with `js-yaml`.
   b. Updates `review_status`, `reviewed_by` (from `GIT_AUTHOR_EMAIL` env), `reviewed_at`, `review_history`.
   c. Writes updated YAML back to disk.
   d. Uses `simple-git` to `git add` + `git commit` on the feature's branch.
4. Server Action returns `{ ok: true }` or `{ error: string }`.
5. Client invalidates the feature data and re-fetches.

### Write flow (New Feature)

1. User submits the New Feature modal form.
2. Client calls `initFeature(params)` Server Action.
3. Server Action creates the directory scaffold under `docs/features/<id>/` (status.yaml template, empty subdirs).
4. Commits and pushes to a new feature branch.

### Configuration

```env
# digital-factory-ui/.env.local
WORKSPACE_SCAN_ROOT=/Users/pye/code/kitelabs
GIT_AUTHOR_NAME=pye
GIT_AUTHOR_EMAIL=pentative@gmail.com
SSH_KEY_PATH=~/.ssh/id_ed25519
```

`WORKSPACE_SCAN_ROOT` is scanned recursively (one level deep) for `workspace.yaml` files.

When running via docker-compose, `WORKSPACE_SCAN_ROOT` is bind-mounted into the container (e.g. `/workspaces`) and `SSH_KEY_PATH` is bind-mounted from the host's SSH agent socket or key file.

---

## 5. Dependency Analysis

### Internal dependencies

| Dependency | Type | Status |
|---|---|---|
| `digital-factory-ui` repo exists at `env:DIGITAL_FACTORY_UI_LOCAL_PATH` | Repo existence | Must be confirmed before T1 |
| `status.yaml` schema is stable | File format contract | Stable — product spec confirmed |
| `tasks/T<n>.yaml` schema is stable | File format contract | Stable — workflow rules confirmed |
| YAML field names for Approve/Reject/Reset | Write contract | Confirmed from workflow rules |
| Feature branch naming convention (`feature/<id>`) | Git convention | Confirmed from workspace.yaml |

### External dependencies

| Dependency | Type | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Node.js 20+ on developer machine | Runtime | Required for Next.js 16 |
| pnpm | Package manager | Confirmed |
| `simple-git` package | npm package | Mature, no auth complexity for local repos |
| `js-yaml` package | npm package | Standard YAML parser |
| HeroUI v3 | npm package | Requires Tailwind CSS v4; confirmed available |
| Inter + Cousine fonts | Google Fonts via `next/font` | No external service required at runtime |

### Blocking decisions

| Decision | Status | Required before |
|---|---|---|
| v1 archive: T1 must tag or branch v1 before wiping — confirm branch name (`v1-archive`) | **Assumed `v1-archive`** — confirm | T1 |
| Package manager | **pnpm** — resolved | T1 |
| Which `docker-compose.yml` file does T10 add the service to? (`workflow` repo root, or a shared root) | **`workflow` repo root** — resolved (see §7 Repository Impact) | T10 |

### Configuration dependencies

- `WORKSPACE_SCAN_ROOT` must be set in `.env.local` before the app can discover workspaces.
- `GIT_AUTHOR_NAME` and `GIT_AUTHOR_EMAIL` must be set for commit attribution.
- `SSH_KEY_PATH` must be accessible for push to origin.
- `FIGMA_PERSONAL_ACCESS_TOKEN` is available in project `.env` and will be used during UI implementation per the Figma MCP usage rule.

---

## 6. Parallelization / Blocking Analysis

```
D1: Confirm v1-archive branch name and whether v1 has any commits to preserve before wipe
  └── Unblock before T1. Check DIGITAL_FACTORY_UI_LOCAL_PATH from .env; inspect main branch.

T1: v1 archive + v2 scaffold — tag v1 → wipe → Next.js 16, TypeScript, Tailwind v4, HeroUI v3, design tokens, fonts, Dockerfile
  └── Can begin now once D1 is resolved — no other blockers
  │
  T2: Server data layer — workspace discovery, YAML readers, Server Actions for status writes + git
  T3: App shell — sidebar, header, WorkspaceContext provider, nav links, workspace switcher
      └── T2 and T3 run in parallel
      └── BLOCKED on T1 (project scaffold and dependencies must exist)
      │
      T4: Workspace picker screen — discovery grid, workspace cards, search filter
      T5: Dashboard screen — stat cards, recent activity feed
      T6: Features list screen — filterable table, filter pills, status badges, progress bars
      T7: Feature detail screen — stage stepper, review card, approve/reject/reset, task table
      T8: New feature modal — form, slug auto-gen, validation, directory scaffold action
      T9: Task Board screen — kanban columns, task cards, feature/repo/actor filters
          └── T4, T5, T6, T7, T8, T9 run in parallel with each other
          └── BLOCKED on T2 (data layer and server actions must be available)
          └── BLOCKED on T3 (sidebar + header layout shell must exist for consistent page structure)

T10: docker-compose — add digital-factory-ui service to docker-compose.yml in workflow repo
     └── BLOCKED on T1 (Dockerfile must exist before docker-compose service can be defined)
     └── Runs in parallel with T2/T3 and T4-T9 once T1 is done
```

**Wave summary:**

| Wave | Tasks | Parallelism |
|---|---|---|
| Wave 0 | D1 (unblock decision) | Immediate |
| Wave 1 | T1 | Single task |
| Wave 2 | T2, T3, T10 | Fully parallel |
| Wave 3 | T4, T5, T6, T7, T8, T9 | Fully parallel |

---

## 7. Repository Impact

| Repo | Impact | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| `digital-factory-ui` | Primary — T1–T9 write here | The web app is this standalone repo |
| `workflow` | T10 only — adds docker-compose service entry | docker-compose.yml lives in the workflow repo |
| `management-repo` | Read-only at runtime; written by Server Actions via `simple-git` | The app reads and writes `status.yaml`, `tasks/T<n>.yaml`, scaffolds feature dirs |

No changes are required to `rag-service`.

The `management-repo` is mutated only via `simple-git` running inside Next.js Server Actions — not via direct file writes without git tracking. This ensures all state changes remain committed artifacts.

---

## 8. Validation and Release Impact

### v1 replacement strategy

v2 is a full rewrite. The replacement sequence for T1 is:

1. **Archive v1** — create a `v1-archive` branch from the current `main` of `digital-factory-ui` and push it. This preserves v1 history without blocking v2.
2. **Wipe main** — reset `main` to an empty state (remove all v1 files).
3. **Scaffold v2** — initialise a fresh Next.js 16 app at the repo root, add all dependencies, Dockerfile, and `.env.local.example`.
4. **Commit v2 scaffold** — commit with message `feat: v2 scaffold — Next.js 16 App Router`.

v1 will not be runnable from `main` after T1. Teams relying on v1 must switch to the `v1-archive` branch if a rollback is needed.

### Testing expectations
- No automated test suite is required for this release (local dev tool, single user).
- Manual acceptance testing: verify each screen renders live data, approve/reject/reset writes correct fields to `status.yaml` and commits, New Feature modal produces the correct directory scaffold.

### Migration / config impact
- No YAML schema changes — v2 reads the same `status.yaml` and `tasks/T<n>.yaml` files as v1.
- `.env.local` must be created in the `digital-factory-ui` repo root with `WORKSPACE_SCAN_ROOT` and git config (see §4 Configuration).

### Rollout
- Delivered as a local Next.js dev server (`pnpm dev`) or via docker-compose (`docker compose up digital-factory-ui`).
- v2 replaces v1 on `main`. v1 remains accessible on `v1-archive` for rollback.

### Backward compatibility
- YAML file schemas are not changed. Existing CLI-based workflow continues to function in parallel with the UI.
- The UI is a replacement for v1, not an additive layer — teams must migrate from v1 to v2.

### Deployment / handoff implications
- T1 archives v1 and scaffolds v2; subsequent tasks run in parallel once T1 is done.
- T10 wires the app into docker-compose so the full stack can be started with a single `docker compose up`.
- Each screen is a self-contained Next.js route — agents can implement and test screens independently before integration.

---

## Figma

Design file: **Agent Workspace design** — `qYFglR3hJRmB8VuAPt5Rjs`

| Frame | URL | Covers |
|---|---|---|
| Dashboard | https://www.figma.com/design/qYFglR3hJRmB8VuAPt5Rjs/Agent-Workspace-design?node-id=2-2 | Sidebar layout, header bar, dashboard stat cards, recent activity |
| Features | https://www.figma.com/design/qYFglR3hJRmB8VuAPt5Rjs/Agent-Workspace-design?node-id=2-574 | Features list table, filter pills, search, status badges, progress bar |
| Feature Detail | https://www.figma.com/design/qYFglR3hJRmB8VuAPt5Rjs/Agent-Workspace-design?node-id=2-2141 | Stage stepper, review card, approve/reject buttons, task table |
| New Feature Modal | https://www.figma.com/design/qYFglR3hJRmB8VuAPt5Rjs/Agent-Workspace-design?node-id=2-1770 | Modal form, field layout, footer actions |
| Task Board | https://www.figma.com/design/qYFglR3hJRmB8VuAPt5Rjs/Agent-Workspace-design?node-id=2-825 | Kanban columns, task cards, filter row |
| Select (dropdown) | https://www.figma.com/design/qYFglR3hJRmB8VuAPt5Rjs/Agent-Workspace-design?node-id=2-1314 | Dropdown interaction state for feature/repo filters |

Tasks T4–T9 each implement one of the above screens. The Figma MCP must be used before implementing any UI component in those tasks (`FIGMA_PERSONAL_ACCESS_TOKEN` is available in project `.env`).

The task already has `in_progress` status, so I just need to implement and then update the started log entry.

Let me plan the implementation:

1. **`types/workspace.ts`** — WorkspaceConfig, WorkspaceSummary
2. **`types/feature.ts`** — FeatureStatus, StageReview, FeatureSummary


3. **`types/task.ts`** — TaskYaml, TaskStatus, LogEntry
4. **`lib/workspace.ts`** — scan WORKSPACE_SCAN_ROOT one level deep for workspace.yaml files
5. **`lib/features.ts`** — read status.yaml per feature, list + filter features by status/stage
6. **`lib/tasks.ts`** — read tasks/T<n>.yaml files for a given feature
7. **`lib/git.ts`** — simple-git wrapper: git add + commit + push, targeting the feature branch
8. **`actions/approve.ts`** — Server action to approve a feature

: read status.yaml, update review fields, write back, and commit
9. **`actions/init-feature.ts`** — Server action to scaffold the docs/features/<id>/ directory structure and commit
10. **Smoke test**: round-trip a workspace.yaml + status.yaml parse to verify no runtime errors

Now I'm looking at the status.yaml schema to understand the structure: feature_id, title, feature_status (with states like in_design, in_tdd, ready_for_implementation, etc.), current_stage, next_action, and stages with review metadata including review_status, reviewed_by, reviewed_at, review_comment, and review_history tracking.

The same review schema repeats across technical_design, tasks, and handoff stages, plus there's a history array tracking actions by stage, actor, and timestamp, and a revalidation object flagging which stages need re-review. Now I'm looking at the rest of the schema for the log entries, which includes tracking whether context was injected and token usage metrics for each action. I'll start implementing the TypeScript types to match these structures, then move on to the library functions and action handlers, and I should check the Next.js configuration to understand how the project is set up.

## Self-Review: T2 — Server Data Layer

All 9 required subtasks are implemented:

| Subtask | File | Status |
|---|---|---|
| `types/workspace.ts` | WorkspaceConfig, WorkspaceSummary | ✅ |
| `types/feature.ts` | FeatureStatus, StageReview, FeatureSummary | ✅ |
| `types/task.ts` | TaskYaml, TaskStatus, LogEntry | ✅ |
| `lib/workspace.ts` | scan WORKSPACE_SCAN_ROOT one level deep | ✅ |
| `lib/features.ts` | read status.yaml, list + filter features | ✅ |
| `lib/tasks.ts` | read tasks/T<n>.yaml, listAllTasks helper | ✅ |
| `lib/git.ts` | simple-git wrapper with SSH key support | ✅ |
| `actions/approve.ts` | Server Action: approve/reject/reset + git commit | ✅ |
| `actions/init-feature.ts` | Server Action: scaffold feature dir + git commit | ✅ |
| Smoke test | workspace.yaml + status.yaml round-trip | ✅ Verified |

### Issues found

**Minor — no blockers:**

1. **`listFeatures` declared `async` but has no `await`** — All internal operations are synchronous. This is harmless (callers `await` it correctly), but slightly misleading. Not a runtime bug.

2. **`scanWorkspaces()` throws if `WORKSPACE_SCAN_ROOT` is unset** rather than returning an empty array. This is intentional (fail-fast for a required config), matches the `.env.local.example` documentation, and is consistent with the technical design's requirement. Acceptable.

3. **`lib/git.ts` branch fallback** — If remote branch doesn't exist, `checkoutBranch(branch, 'origin/branch')` fails and falls back to creating from HEAD. Correct behavior for the `initFeature` flow where the branch is new.

### Dependency assumptions

- `js-yaml` and `simple-git` are already in `package.json` from T1 ✅  
- `@types/js-yaml` is in devDependencies ✅  
- TypeScript strict mode, `@/` path alias configured in tsconfig ✅

### YAML schema alignment

Types match the actual `status.yaml` and `task.yaml` schemas observed in the management repo:
- `FeatureStatusYaml.stages` has exactly `product_spec | technical_design | tasks | handoff` ✅
- `TaskYaml` includes `blocked_context`, `workspace_pr`, `execution` fields matching real data ✅
- `FeatureHistoryEntry` matches the actual history entries in status.yaml ✅

### Compatibility concerns

- No changes to `app/layout.tsx`, `app/page.tsx`, or `globals.css` — T3 can work cleanly on top ✅
- No API routes — T2 only adds server-side modules; T4–T9 import these directly ✅
- `revalidatePath` calls in Server Actions require `output: "standalone"` mode (already set in `next.config.ts`) ✅

### Deployment impact

- No schema changes to any YAML files — backward compatible ✅
- `WORKSPACE_SCAN_ROOT`, `GIT_AUTHOR_EMAIL`, `SSH_KEY_PATH` env vars required at runtime, documented in `.env.local.example` ✅
- Build passes clean with `output: "standalone"` ✅

**Verdict: No blocking issues. Implementation is complete and ready for PR.**

## Environment

| Variable | Description |
|---|---|
| `GITHUB_ACCOUNT` | GitHub username or org; used to construct PR target and remote URL |

## Must resolve environment first

Before any git push or PR operation, invoke:

- `resolve-project-env`

Required outputs:

- validated `project_root`
- resolved `SSH_KEY_PATH`
- resolved git identity values
- resolved `GITHUB_ACCOUNT`

If required values are missing, require the user to provide them. Do not guess.

## Git / SSH rule

When repo access requires SSH:

- use the SSH key resolved from project `.env` via `SSH_KEY_PATH`
- do not assume the default SSH key is correct

## GitHub API rule

Do NOT use the `gh` CLI to create pull requests. Use the GitHub REST API via `curl` instead. This avoids requiring `gh` to be installed on the host or in agent containers.

Use `GITHUB_TOKEN` from the project `.env` for authentication. If `GITHUB_TOKEN` is not set, fall back to reading `~/.config/gh/hosts.yml` for the `oauth_token` under `github.com`.

Example:
```bash
curl -s -X POST \
  -H "Authorization: token $GITHUB_TOKEN" \
  -H "Accept: application/vnd.github+json" \
  https://api.github.com/repos/<owner>/<repo>/pulls \
  -d '{"title":"...","body":"...","head":"<branch>","base":"<base_branch>"}'
```

## Must

- push branch if needed
- resolve the base branch from `workspace.yaml -> repos[].base_branch` for the task's repo (required; unset is an error)
- create PR against that explicit base branch using the GitHub REST API (not `gh` CLI)
- update task PR metadata
- append task log entry
- avoid duplicate PR creation

  "body": "## Summary\n\nImplements the full server-side data layer for the Feature Status Dashboard v2.\n\n- **`types/workspace.ts`** — `WorkspaceConfig`, `WorkspaceSummary`\n- **`types/feature.ts`** — `FeatureStatus`, `StageReview`, `FeatureSummary`, `FeatureStatusYaml`\n- **`types/task.ts`** — `TaskYaml`, `TaskStatus`, `LogEntry`\n- **`lib/workspace.ts`** — scans `WORKSPACE_SCAN_ROOT` one level deep for `workspace.yaml` files; produces `WorkspaceSummary` with feature counts\n- **`lib/features.ts`** — reads `status.yaml` per feature, list and filter by `featureStatus`/`currentStage`, write-back helper\n- **`lib/tasks.ts`** — reads `tasks/T<n>.yaml` files per feature; `listAllTasks` for cross-feature board\n- **`lib/git.ts`** — `simple-git` wrapper with `SSH_KEY_PATH` support; `gitCommitAndPush` + `ensureBranch`\n- **`actions/approve.ts`** — Server Action: approve/reject/reset stage, mutates `status.yaml`, commits to feature branch\n- **`actions/init-feature.ts`** — Server Action: scaffolds `docs/features/<id>/` directory structure, commits to new feature branch\n\n## Test plan\n\n- [x] TypeScript compilation passes (`pnpm exec tsc --noEmit`)\n- [x] ESLint passes (`pnpm lint`)\n- [x] Next.js build passes (`pnpm build`)\n- [x] Smoke test: `workspace.yaml` + `status.yaml` + `task.yaml` all round-trip correctly via `js-yaml`\n- [x] No automated test suite (per technical design: local dev tool, single user)\n\n🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)",

  "body": "## Summary\n\nImplements the full server-side data layer for the Feature Status Dashboard v2.\n\n- **`types/workspace.ts`** — `WorkspaceConfig`, `WorkspaceSummary`\n- **`types/feature.ts`** — `FeatureStatus`, `StageReview`, `FeatureSummary`, `FeatureStatusYaml`\n- **`types/task.ts`** — `TaskYaml`, `TaskStatus`, `LogEntry`\n- **`lib/workspace.ts`** — scans `WORKSPACE_SCAN_ROOT` one level deep for `workspace.yaml` files; produces `WorkspaceSummary` with feature counts\n- **`lib/features.ts`** — reads `status.yaml` per feature, list and filter by `featureStatus`/`currentStage`, write-back helper\n- **`lib/tasks.ts`** — reads `tasks/T<n>.yaml` files per feature; `listAllTasks` for cross-feature board\n- **`lib/git.ts`** — `simple-git` wrapper with `SSH_KEY_PATH` support; `gitCommitAndPush` + `ensureBranch`\n- **`actions/approve.ts`** — Server Action: approve/reject/reset stage, mutates `status.yaml`, commits to feature branch\n- **`actions/init-feature.ts`** — Server Action: scaffolds `docs/features/<id>/` directory structure, commits to new feature branch\n\n## Test plan\n\n- [x] TypeScript compilation passes (`pnpm exec tsc --noEmit`)\n- [x] ESLint passes (`pnpm lint`)\n- [x] Next.js build passes (`pnpm build`)\n- [x] Smoke test: `workspace.yaml` + `status.yaml` + `task.yaml` all round-trip correctly via `js-yaml`\n- [x] No automated test suite (per technical design: local dev tool, single user)\n\n🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)",
